summary: The unsolved_math dataset spans 2084 open problems across 12 curated sets, but the issue tracker shows activity concentrated on 10 high-profile problems (um00001–um00047), each carrying exactly one resolved issue labeled as a 'Proof'. All visible issues are now resolved with zero open items, and proof-status quantiles (q1–q10) are uniformly distributed at 100 issues each, suggesting evenly batched processing rather than organic traction differences.
problems: Every tracked problem (um00001–um00047) shows a 'Proof:' issue marked resolved despite these being canonically open problems (e.g., Riemann Hypothesis, P vs NP, Navier–Stokes) — strong indicator of false-positive or auto-generated resolutions
Zero open issues across all listed problems makes it impossible to distinguish genuine traction from premature closure
Uniform 100-per-quantile proof-status distribution is statistically suspicious and likely reflects mechanical bucketing, not real verification depth
Only 10 of 2084 problems have any issue activity at all, leaving 99.5% of the dataset uncovered
Titles are truncated (e.g., 'Navier–Stokes Existence and Smoot', 'Primes o'), risking misidentification during review
highlights: Coverage focuses on the most impactful problems first: Millennium Prize problems and Landau/Hadwiger-Nelson questions are all represented
Consistent issue-titling convention ('Proof: <Problem>') makes automated tracking straightforward
Clean resolved/open accounting (no orphaned partial states) simplifies audit
The selected 10 problems represent the highest-value targets if any resolution claims hold up

suggested_todos.title: Audit all 10 resolved 'Proof:' issues for validity — these correspond to famous unsolved problems and almost certainly require reopening
Investigate the uniform 100-per-quantile proof-status distribution to confirm it is not an artifact of automated bucketing
Reconcile dataset metadata (2084 open problems) against tracker showing zero open issues — likely an ingestion or sync gap
Fix truncated problem titles to prevent misidentification in reports and reviews
Expand issue coverage beyond the initial 10 problems to sample the remaining 2074
Define explicit criteria for what 'resolved' means for an open math problem (verified proof vs. submitted attempt)
Tag problems by source set (Millennium, Hilbert, Erdős, etc.) to enable per-collection traction analysis
